html:
VII

On the following day there was much feasting amongst the Romany chals of Mr.¬ÝPetulengro‚Äôs party.249 Throughout the forenoon the Romany chies did scarcely anything but cook flesh, and the flesh which they cooked was swine‚Äôs flesh. About two o‚Äôclock, the chals and chies, dividing themselves into various parties, sat down and partook of the fare, which was partly roasted, partly sodden. I dined that day with Mr.¬ÝPetulengro and his wife and family, Ursula, Mr.¬Ýand Mrs.¬ÝChikno, and Sylvester and his two children. Sylvester, it will be as well to say, was a widower, and had consequently no one to cook his victuals for him, supposing he had any, which was not always the case, Sylvester‚Äôs affairs being seldom in a prosperous state. He was noted for his bad success in trafficking, notwithstanding the many hints which he received from Jasper, under whose protection he had placed himself, even as Tawno Chikno had done, who himself, as the reader has heard on a former occasion, was anything but a wealthy subject, though he was at all times better off than Sylvester, the Lazarus of the Romany tribe.

All our party ate with a good appetite, except myself, who, feeling rather melancholy that day, had little desire to eat. I did not, like the others, partake of the pork, but got my dinner entirely off the body of a squirrel which had been shot the day before by a chal of the name of Piramus,250 who, besides being a good shot, was celebrated for his skill in playing on the fiddle. During the dinner a horn filled with ale passed frequently around; I drank of it more than once, and felt inspirited by the draughts. The repast concluded, Sylvester and his children departed to their tent, and Mr.¬ÝPetulengro, Tawno and myself, getting up, went and lay down under a shady hedge, where Mr.¬ÝPetulengro, lighting his pipe, began to smoke, and where Tawno presently fell asleep. I was about to fall asleep also, when I heard the sound of music and song. Piramus was playing on the fiddle, whilst Mrs.¬ÝChikno, who had a voice of her own, was singing in tones sharp enough, but of great power, a gipsy song:251‚ÅÝ‚Äî

Poisoning the Porker

By Mrs.¬ÝChikno

To mande shoon ye Romany chals
Who besh in the pus about the yag,
I‚Äôll pen how we drab the baulo,
I‚Äôll pen how we drab the baulo.

We jaws to the drab-engro ker,
Trin hors-worth there of drab we lels,
And when to the swety back we wels
We pens we‚Äôll drab the baulo,
We‚Äôll have a drab at the baulo.

And then we kairs the drab opr√©,
And then we jaws to the farming ker,
To mang a beti habben,
A beti poggado habben.

A rinkeno baulo there we dick,
And then we pens in Romano jib:
‚ÄúWust lis odoi opr√© ye chick,
And the baulo he will lel lis,
The baulo he will lel lis.‚Äù

Coliko, coliko saulo we
Apopli to the farming ker
Will wel and mang him mullo,
Will wel and mang his truppo.

And so we kairs, and so we kairs;
The baulo in the rarde mers;
We mang him on the saulo,
And rig to the tan the baulo.

And then we toves the wendror well
Till sore the wendror iuziou se,
Till kekkeno drab‚Äôs adrey lis,
Till drab there‚Äôs kek adrey lis.

And then his truppo well we hatch,
Kin levinor at the kitchema,
And have a kosko habben,
A kosko Romano habben.

The boshom engro kils, he kils,
The tawnie juva gils, she gils
A puro Romano gillie,
Now shoon the Romano gillie.

Which song I had translated in the following manner, in my younger days, for a lady‚Äôs album:‚ÅÝ‚Äî

Listen to me ye Roman lads, who are seated in the straw about the fire, and I will tell how we poison the porker, I will tell how we poison the porker.

We go to the house of the poisonmonger,252 where we buy three pennies‚Äô worth of bane, and when we return to our people, we say we will poison the porker; we will try and poison the porker.

We then make up the poison, and then we take our way to the house of the farmer, as if to beg a bit of victuals, a little broken victuals.

We see a jolly porker, and then we say in Roman language, ‚ÄúFling the bane yonder amongst the dirt, and the porker soon will find it, the porker soon will find it.‚Äù

Early on the morrow, we will return to the farmhouse, and beg the dead porker, the body of the dead porker.

And so we do, even so we do; the porker dieth during the night; on the morrow we beg the porker, and carry to the tent the porker.

And then we wash the inside well, till all the inside is perfectly clean, till there‚Äôs no bane within it, not a poison grain within it.

And then we roast the body well, send for ale to the alehouse, and have a merry banquet, a merry Roman banquet.

The fellow with the fiddle plays, he plays; the little lassie sings, she sings an ancient Roman ditty; now hear the Roman ditty.

Song of the Broken Chastity

By Ursula

Penn‚Äôd the Romany chi k√© laki dye,
‚ÄúMiry dearie dye mi shom cambri!‚Äù
‚ÄúAnd coin kerdo tute cambri,
Miry dearie chi, miry Romany chi?‚Äù
‚ÄúO miry dye a boro rye,
A bovalo rye, a gorgiko rye,
Sos kistur pr√© a pellengo grye,
‚ÄôTwas yov sos kerdo man cambri.‚Äù
‚ÄúTu tawnie vassavie lubbeny,
Tu chal from miry tan abri;
Had a Romany chal kair‚Äôd tute cambri,
Then I had penn‚Äôd ke tute chie,
But tu shan a vassavie lubbeny
With gorgikie rat to be cambri.‚Äù

‚ÄúThere‚Äôs some kernel in those songs, brother,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝPetulengro, when the songs and music were over.

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said I; ‚Äúthey are certainly very remarkable songs. [I translated both long ago for a lady‚Äôs album.‚Äù

‚ÄúA lady‚Äôs what, brother?‚Äù

‚ÄúA lil in which she kept bits of song and poetry to show occasionally to young ladies.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou had no right to do that, brother; you had no right to let the ladies into the secrets of people who took you up when you were little better than half a fool. But what did the lady say to them?‚Äù

‚ÄúAs I have done just now, that they were remarkable songs, strongly expressive of the manners and peculiarities of a remarkable people.‚Äù

‚ÄúBrother, she was a gentlewoman, and I forgive you.‚Äù]

‚ÄúI say, Jasper, I hope you have not been drabbing baulor lately.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd suppose we have, brother, what then?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, it is a very dangerous practice, to say nothing of the wickedness of it.‚Äù

‚ÄúNecessity has no law, brother.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat is true,‚Äù said I; ‚ÄúI have always said so, but you are not necessitous, and should not drab baulor.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd who told you we had been drabbing baulor?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, you have had a banquet of pork, and after the banquet, Mrs.¬ÝChikno sang a song about drabbing baulor, so I naturally thought you might have lately been engaged in such a thing.‚Äù

‚ÄúBrother, you occasionally utter a word or two of common sense. It was natural for you to suppose, after seeing that dinner of pork, and hearing that song, that we had been drabbing baulor; I will now tell you that we have not been doing so. What have you to say to that?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat I am very glad of it.‚Äù

‚ÄúHad you tasted that pork, brother, you would have found that it was sweet and tasty, which balluva that is drabbed can hardly be expected to be. We have no reason to drab baulor at present, we have money and credit; but necessity has no law. Our forefathers occasionally drabbed baulor; some of our people may still do such a thing, but only from compulsion.‚Äù

‚ÄúI see,‚Äù said I; ‚Äúand at your merry meetings you sing songs upon the compulsatory deeds of your people, alias, their villainous actions; and, after all, what would the stirring poetry of any nation be, but for its compulsatory deeds? Look at the poetry of Scotland, the heroic part, founded almost entirely on the villainous deeds of the Scotch nation; cow-stealing, for example, which is very little better than drabbing baulor; whilst the softer part is mostly about the slips of its females among the broom, so that no upholder of Scotch poetry could censure Ursula‚Äôs song253 as indelicate, even if he understood it. What do you think, Jasper?‚Äù

‚ÄúI think, brother, as I before said, that occasionally you utter a word of common sense; you were talking of the Scotch, brother; what do you think of a Scotchman finding fault with Romany?‚Äù

‚ÄúA Scotchman finding fault with Romany, Jasper! Oh dear, but you joke, the thing could never be.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, and at Piramus‚Äôs fiddle; what do you think of a Scotchman turning up his nose at Piramus‚Äôs fiddle?‚Äù

‚ÄúA Scotchman turning up his nose at Piramus‚Äôs fiddle! nonsense, Jasper.‚Äù

‚ÄúDo you know what I most dislike, brother?‚Äù

‚ÄúI do not, unless it be the constable, Jasper.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is not the constable; it‚Äôs a beggar on horseback, brother.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat do you mean by a beggar on horseback?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, a scamp, brother, raised above his proper place, who takes every opportunity of giving himself fine airs. About a week ago, my people and myself camped on a green by a plantation in the neighbourhood of a great house. In the evening we were making merry, the girls were dancing, while Piramus was playing on the fiddle a tune of his own composing, to which he has given his own name, Piramus of Rome, and which is much celebrated amongst our people, and from which I have been told that one of the grand gorgio composers, who once heard it, has taken several hints. So, as we were making merry, a great many grand people, lords and ladies, I believe, came from the great house, and looked on, as the girls danced to the tune of Piramus of Rome, and seemed much pleased; and when the girls had left off dancing, and Piramus playing, the ladies wanted to have their fortunes told; so I bade Mikailia Chikno, who can tell a fortune when she pleases better than anyone else, tell them a fortune, and she, being in a good mind, told them a fortune which pleased them very much. So, after they had heard their fortunes, one of them asked if any of our women could sing; and I told them several could, more particularly Leviathan‚ÅÝ‚Äîyou know Leviathan, she is not here now, but some miles distant; she is our best singer, Ursula coming next. So the lady said she should like to hear Leviathan sing, whereupon Leviathan sang the Gudlo pesham, and Piramus played the tune of the same name, which as you know, means the honeycomb, the song and the tune being well entitled to the name, being wonderfully sweet. Well, everybody present seemed mighty well pleased with the song and music, with the exception of one person, a carroty-haired Scotch body; how he came there I don‚Äôt know, but there he was; and, coming forward, he began in Scotch as broad as a barn-door to find fault with the music and the song, saying, that he had never heard viler stuff than either. Well, brother, out of consideration for the civil gentry with whom the fellow had come, I held my peace for a long time, and in order to get the subject changed, I said to Mikailia in Romany, you have told the ladies their fortunes, now tell the gentlemen theirs, quick, quick‚ÅÝ‚Äîpen lende dukkerin. Well, brother, the Scotchman, I suppose, thinking I was speaking ill of him, fell into a greater passion than before, and catching hold of the word dukkerin‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚ÄòDukkerin,‚Äô said he, ‚Äòwhat‚Äôs dukkerin?‚Äô ‚ÄòDukkerin,‚Äô said I, ‚Äòis fortune, a man or woman‚Äôs destiny; don‚Äôt you like the word?‚Äô ‚ÄòWord! d‚Äôye ca‚Äô that a word? a bonnie word,‚Äô said he. ‚ÄòPerhaps you‚Äôll tell us what it is in Scotch,‚Äô said I, ‚Äòin order that we may improve our language by a Scotch word; a pal of mine has told me that we have taken a great many words from foreign lingos.‚Äô ‚ÄòWhy, then, if that be the case, fellow, I will tell you; it is e‚Äôen ‚Äúspaeing,‚Äù‚Ää‚Äô said he very seriously. ‚ÄòWell, then,‚Äô said I, ‚ÄòI‚Äôll keep my own word, which is much the prettiest‚ÅÝ‚Äîspaeing! spaeing! why, I should be ashamed to make use of the word, it sounds so much like a certain other word;‚Äô and then I made a face as if I were unwell. ‚ÄòPerhaps it‚Äôs Scotch also for that?‚Äô ‚ÄòWhat do ye mean by speaking in that guise to a gentleman?‚Äô said he, ‚Äòyou insolent vagabond, without a name or a country.‚Äô ‚ÄòThere you are mistaken,‚Äô said I; ‚Äòmy country is Egypt, but we ‚ÄôGyptians, like you Scotch, are rather fond of travelling; and as for name‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy name is Jasper Petulengro, perhaps you have a better; what is it?‚Äô ‚ÄòSandy Macraw.‚Äô At that, brother, the gentlemen burst into a roar of laughter, and all the ladies tittered.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou were rather severe on the Scotchman, Jasper.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot at all, brother, and suppose I were, he began first; I am the civilest man in the world, and never interfere with anybody, who lets me and mine alone. He finds fault with Romany, forsooth! why, L‚ÅÝ‚∏∫‚ÅÝd A‚Äômighty, what‚Äôs Scotch? He doesn‚Äôt like our songs; what are his own? I understand them as little as he mine; I have heard one or two of them, and pretty rubbish they seemed. But the best of the joke is, the fellow‚Äôs finding fault with Piramus‚Äôs fiddle‚ÅÝ‚Äîa chap from the land of bagpipes finding fault with Piramus‚Äôs fiddle! Why, I‚Äôll back that fiddle against all the bagpipes in Scotland, and Piramus against all the bagpipers; for though Piramus weighs but ten stone, he shall flog a Scotchman of twenty.‚Äù

‚ÄúScotchmen are never so fat as that,‚Äù said I, ‚Äúunless, indeed, they have been a long time pensioners of England. I say, Jasper, what remarkable names your people have!‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd what pretty names, brother; there‚Äôs my own for example, Jasper; then there‚Äôs Ambrose and Sylvester;254 then there‚Äôs Culvato, which signifies Claude; then there‚Äôs Piramus‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs a nice name, brother.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen there‚Äôs your wife‚Äôs name, Pakomovna; then there‚Äôs Ursula and Morella.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen, brother, there‚Äôs Ercilla.‚Äù

‚ÄúErcilla! the name of the great poet of Spain, how wonderful; then Leviathan.‚Äù

‚ÄúThe name of a ship, brother; Leviathan was named after a ship, so don‚Äôt make a wonder out of her. But there‚Äôs Sanpriel255 and Synfye.‚Äù256

‚ÄúAy, and Clementina and Lavinia, Camillia and Lydia, Curlanda and Orlanda; wherever did they get those names?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhere did my wife get her necklace, brother?‚Äù

‚ÄúShe knows best, Jasper. I hope‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúCome, no hoping! She got it from her grandmother, who died at the age of 103, and sleeps in Coggeshall churchyard. She got it from her mother, who also died very old, and who could give no other account of it than that it had been in the family time out of mind.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhence could they have got it?‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy, perhaps where they got their names, brother. A gentleman, who had travelled much, once told me that he had seen the sister of it about the neck of an Indian queen.‚Äù

‚ÄúSome of your names, Jasper, appear to be church names; your own, for example, and Ambrose, and Sylvester;257 perhaps you got them from the Papists, in the times of Popery; but where did you get such a name as Piramus, a name of Grecian romance? Then some of them appear to be Slavonian; for example, Mikailia and Pakomovna. I don‚Äôt know much of Slavonian; but‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat is Slavonian, brother?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe family name of certain nations, the principal of which is the Russian, and from which the word slave is originally derived. You have heard of the Russians, Jasper?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, brother, and seen some. I saw their crallis at the time of the peace; he was not a bad-looking man for a Russian.‚Äù

‚ÄúBy the by, Jasper, I‚Äôm half-inclined to think that crallis is a Slavish word. I saw something like it in a lil called Voltaire‚Äôs Life of Charles.258 How you should have come by such names and words is to me incomprehensible.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou seem posed, brother.‚Äù

‚ÄúI really know very little about you, Jasper.‚Äù

‚ÄúVery little indeed, brother. We know very little about ourselves; and you know nothing, save what we have told you; and we have now and then told you things about us which are not exactly true, simply to make a fool of you, brother. You will say that was wrong; perhaps it was. Well, Sunday will be here in a day or two, when we will go to church, where possibly we shall hear a sermon on the disastrous consequences of lying.‚Äù
